figures.caption: Table 5 Task-specific commit predicates. All ϵ are fixed constants in code/config. Predicates are evaluated only after attempt is triggered (gated by Eq. 3 / gating rule above).
Figure 1 Safe-only fine-tuning enables VLA to generalize action trajectories, but capability does not imply safety.

Figure 5 Task-level effect of the Safety Option Layer (SOL) on π0. We compare success rates on safe and unsafe twins under three settings: Baseline, L1 (attribute-based), and L2 (VLM-based). L1 acts as a strong rule-based upper bound in our controlled setting, while L2 provides uneven but informative hazard suppression: it reduces unsafe execution on several tasks, yet remains category-dependent rather than constituting a complete policy-level safety solution.
Table 4 Training hyperparameters for VLA baselines. All models are fine-tuned on the same 600 SAFE-only demonstrations.

Table 1 Performance generalization across safe/unsafe twins. Results show that fine-tuning on safe-only data leads to a consistent capability-driven trend: as models become more adept at task execution (higher SRsafe), they increasingly execute hazardous requests in matched unsafe twins (higher SRunsafe). This highlights a fundamental challenge where manipulation proficiency scales faster than contextual safety awareness in the absence of explicit negative samples.

Table 2 Detailed stage-wise metrics across all tasks. We report the attempt, commit (pre-IPE), and success rates for both safe and unsafe twin scenarios. The results highlight that high capability in safe tasks often correlates with high hazardous progression in unsafe twins.

Figure 2 Overview of HazardArena. We extend assets and define a risk taxonomy to instantiate seed-matched safe/unsafe twins that hold physical interaction constant while varying semantic permissibility. Expert trajectories are collected only in safe twins and exported in standard formats (RLDS, LeRobot) for training and evaluation.
Table 9 Selected safety-risk tasks used in our evaluation.

Figure 4 Stage-wise event rates reveal unsafe progression beyond endpoint success. We report attempt, commit (pre-IPE critical configuration), and terminal success rates. In unsafe twins, commit_rate is often substantially higher than success_rate, indicating hazardous near-completions that fail brittle terminal predicates.

Figure 3 Twin scenarios with stage events (example: insert outlet). We show two SAFE twins (top/middle) and their matched UNSAFE twin (bottom) generated under the same physical layout and motor requirements. Colored stars mark the first timestep of each stage event: attempt (blue, task engagement), grasp (green, optional), commit (orange, reaching the pre-IPE critical configuration), and success (red). The matched UNSAFE episode differs only in risk-critical semantics (actor/target choice and permissibility), while the physical interaction program and motor requirements remain comparable.

Table 6 SOL-L1 as auditable semantic rules (subset used in our six evaluated tasks). Each rule matches actor/target attributes and an interaction type; any match triggers FREEZE pre-commit. Partial support indicates the attribute exists but is not the primary interface or not provided in a scalable/standardized form.
